macro_segments.title: Placing a Sieve on a Vessel on Yom Tov: Rabbi Eliezer's Permissive View and the Debate Over Its Classification

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and the Chachamim Regarding Placing a Sieve on Top of a Jar
Framing the Issue: Is It a Temporary Ohel (Tent) That Permits Rabbi Eliezer's View Through Machshirei Ochel Nefesh
The Gemara's Question: What Is the Halakhic Status if One Actually Places the Sieve on Yom Tov—Is It Biblically or Rabbinically Prohibited
Rav Yosef's Position: Placing the Sieve Incurs a Sin Offering, Suggesting a Biblical Violation
Abaye's Challenge: Questioning Whether This Truly Constitutes the Melacha of Ohel Given Its Trivial Nature
Abaye's Resolution: The Prohibition Is Rabbinical, Not an Actual Ohel but Rather Uvda D'Chol (Weekday Activity)

macro_segments.title: The Compilation of Braytos About Temporary Structures: Distinguishing Permitted and Prohibited Cases of Ohel Creation

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Organized Teachings: Four Prohibited Structures (God, Mishmeret, Kila, Chisei Galen) and Their Rabbinic Status
The Distinction Between Temporary Structures Without a Tefach Roof and Permanent Ohels: Status of Sin Offering Liability
Permitted Structures: Bed, Folding Chair, and Toilet Seat—Cases Where the Ohel Is Already Assembled and Only Requires Unfolding
The Principle of Already-Made Ohels: Items on Hinges or Pre-assembled Require Only Opening, Not Creation

macro_segments.title: Sifting Wine: The Melacha Classification as Meraqed or Borer and the Nature of Separation

micro_segments.title: The Dispute Over Placing Wine in a Sieve and Whether Doing So Incurs a Sin Offering
Rav Sheshes's Challenge: How Can a Rabbinical Prohibition and a Biblical Sin Offering Coexist in One Dispute
The Parallel of the Golden Tiara: Rabbi Meir and the Chachamim Present Opposing Halakhic Conclusions on Identical Issues
Raba's Position: The Sieving of Wine Constitutes Borer Because Food Is Removed While Refuse Is Left Behind
Rabbi Zeira's Position: The Sieving of Wine Constitutes Meraqed Because the Refuse Rises to the Top and Food Falls Below
The Gemara's Analysis: Why Meraqed Is More Analogous Than Borer for Wine Sifting; Distinction in Liquids vs. Solids

macro_segments.title: The Tallit Folded as a Temporary Canopy: Conditions for Permitting Unfurling and the Role of Drawstrings

micro_segments.title: The Case of a Folded Tallit Stretched Over Posts as a Temporary Canopy: Rabbinical Prohibition but No Sin Offering
The Permissive Factor: A String or Drawstring Attached Allows Unfurling Lechatchila Because the First Tefach Is Pre-made
Tosafos's Distinction: The Requirement of a First Tefach Already Constructed Before Spreading Over Additional Area

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Canopies and Beds: Clarifying Which Temporary Structures Are Permitted and Which Are Forbidden

micro_segments.title: The Playful Exchange Between Rav Kahana and Rav: Distinguishing Permission for Beds from Prohibition of Canopies
The Carmonite Bed: A Special Case Involving Snapping Legs Into Place, Raising Concerns of Boneh and Makkeh B'Patish
The Evidence of Rav Yosef: Canopies in Rav Huna's House Were Spread in Evening and Thrown Down in Morning
Curtains and Temporary Walls: Rav's Teaching That Hanging a Vilon (Curtain) Is Permitted Because Walls Are Distinct from Roofs
Wedding Canopies (Kilas Chasanim): Permitted Because They Come to a Point Without a Tefach-Wide Roof, Making Them Walls Rather Than Ohels
Rav Sheshes's Clarification: If the Wedding Canopy Has a Tefach-Wide Roof at the Top, It Becomes Forbidden; Further Refinements of the Three-Tefach Rule

macro_segments.title: The Hat (Siana) and Clothing as Temporary Ohels: Determining When Wearing Protective Coverings Violates Shabbat

micro_segments.title: The Felt Hat (Siana): Permitted Because the Entire Structure Is Pre-made, Similar to Other Hinged Objects
The Debate on Hat Brims: Whether a Siana Is Forbidden if It Has a Tefach-Wide Brim Serving as a Roof
The Cloak Over the Head: If Not Held Tightly, Forbidden Due to Fear of Carrying in Reshut Harabbim; If Tight, Permitted
Tosafos's Alternative: The Issue May Be Whether a Stiff Brim Creates an Appearance of an Ohel, Making It Forbidden

macro_segments.title: Rami bar Yechezkel's Letter to Rav Huna: Two Teachings on Shabbat Law and a Digression Into the Future Forgetting of Torah

micro_segments.title: The God (Leather Flask) and the Oven Leg: Two Shabbat Teachings Transmitted in the Name of Rav via Rami bar Yechezkel
The God When Held by Straps: Permitted on Shabbat Only if Two People Spread It; One Person Creates Tightness and Appearance of Ohel
The Kila (Proper Roof Structure): Forbidden Even With Ten People Because It Cannot Avoid Being Stretched, Making It a True Ohel
The Oven with One Slipped Leg: Permitted to Move When One Leg Is Off, But Forbidden When Two or More Legs Have Slipped
Rava's Stringent View: Even One Slipped Leg Is Forbidden Due to Fear of Fixing It, Which Would Constitute Boneh or Makkeh B'Patish

macro_segments.title: The Agadic Teaching on Torah: Rav's Prophecy That Torah Will Be Forgotten and the Rabbis' Response at Kerem B'Yavne

micro_segments.title: Rav's Statement: There Will Come a Time When Torah Is Forgotten from Israel, Based on the Verse About Increased Smitings
The Context at Kerem B'Yavne: The Rabbis' Fear of Torah Being Lost After the Churban, Motivating the Consolidation of Testimony
The Sign of Forgotten Torah: A Woman Will Carry a Terumah Loaf and Search Synagogues Seeking to Know If It Is Pure or Impure
The Gemara's Challenge: How Can Basic Laws of Purity Be Forgotten When They Are Explicitly Stated in Scripture
The Resolution: What Will Be Forgotten Is Not the Basic Categories but the Precise Mechanisms of How Tumah Transmits Through Vessels
Rabbi Shimon ben Yochai's Counter-View: Torah Will Never Be Completely Forgotten, But Clear, Unified Teachings Will Be Lacking

topical_tags.term: Machshirei Ochel Nefesh (Food Preparation on Yom Tov)
